okoarc.title: Ökoarc (Ecological Self)
okoarc.description: The "Ökoarc" is the individual seeker who has set out on the path of sustainability and awareness. This term refers to a person who recognises that they are part of nature and that their decisions affect the environment.
okoarc.focus: Transforming one's own lifestyle, learning, experimenting.

koma.title: Koma (Community Member)
koma.description: The "Koma" is no longer just on an individual path, but is an active member of a community. The original meaning of the word (friend, comrade, godparent) refers to deep, trust-based human relationships.

matchmaking.title: Ally Finder
matchmaking.description: Recommendations based on skills, energy levels, causes, and RDG areas.
matchmaking.noMatchesDesc: There is not enough data in your profile.
matchmaking.scoreTransparencyTitle: What the score can and cannot see
matchmaking.scoreTransparencyBody: Scores use profile offers, needs, causes, RDG areas, availability, language overlap, and recent profile freshness. They do not verify trust, lived context, seasonal timing, land or community lineage, or whether the other person has capacity today. Treat the score as a starting point, not a ranking of people.
matchmaking.scoreAria: Match score {{score}}. This is a routing hint, not a ranking of the person.
matchmaking.firstExchangeBody: Before asking for help, name one thing you can offer, carry, or make easier in this exchange.

errors.authRequired: Log in to use Ally Finder.

matchmaking.whyGoodMatch: Why this could be a good match
matchmaking.scoreDisclosureTitle: what this score does and does not see
matchmaking.scoreDisclosureBody: This recommendation uses declared role labels, offers, needs, causes, language, availability, recency, and public profile context. It does not see private intent, lived trust, offline obligations, or whether a conversation will feel mutual. Some protective dampening is governed and aggregate, but never exposed as a per-user leverage label.

privacy.title: Privacy Settings
privacy.metadataDescription: GDPR rights: restrict processing, opt out of algorithmic matching, and schedule account deletion.
privacy.intro: Use these controls to exercise your GDPR rights. Changes take effect immediately.
privacy.policyPrefix: Read more about data handling in the
privacy.policySuffix: or contact our privacy contact at

processing.title: Restrict data processing
processing.description: GDPR Article 18 - You can ask analytics and recommendation features to stop while keeping access to your account. This restriction does not affect processing required for core operation, such as sign-in and email notifications.

matching.title: Algorithmic matching
matching.description: GDPR Article 22 - Changemappers uses algorithmic systems to recommend community connections and opportunities based on interests, availability, and location. You can opt out of automatic matching and still browse other users manually.
matching.sensitiveActive: Explicit consent is active for matching that uses archetypes, needs, causes, and functional profile signals.
matching.sensitiveInactive: Sensitive-data matching is not active for this account.
matching.profileVisibilityNote: Profile, search, and map visibility controls limit what others can see; they do not by themselves disable algorithmic matching. Disable automatic matching or withdraw sensitive matching consent here.

deletion.title: Delete account
deletion.descriptionPre: GDPR Article 17 - After account deletion, your personal data will be anonymized. You have
deletion.descriptionPost: to reverse the decision after the request is received. After 30 days, deletion is permanent and cannot be undone.
deletion.scheduled: Deletion scheduled: {{date}}. The account will be permanently deleted after this date.

roleLabels.title: Role-label use
roleLabels.description: Archetypes and role labels are contextual signals, not identity claims. They help route attention only when you allow matching and sensitive profile signals to be used.
roleLabels.currentControl: V1 control: turn off automatic matching or withdraw sensitive matching consent to stop role labels from being used in recommendation scoring. More granular per-label controls remain planned work.

export.title: Download your data
export.description: Export a machine-readable copy of your account, profile, communication, activity, and rights-management data.

cookies.title: Cookie settings
cookies.description: Reopen cookie preferences to withdraw optional analytics consent at any time.

privacy.policyFallback: If email delivery is unavailable, use the in-app Feedback button and start the message with "privacy request".

favoritesPage.title: Favorites
favoritesPage.description: Your saved people, pages, and opportunities will appear here as favorite actions are added across the platform.
favoritesPage.emptyDescription: Start from the feed and keep track of the updates you want to revisit.

settingsPage.privacyDescription: Profile visibility, federation sharing, data export, and account deletion controls live in your profile settings tab.
settingsPage.cmapReviewDescription: Review your Arrival, Settling, or Stewardship surfaces manually. Automated Day-14, Day-90, and annual prompts are deferred in V1.

settingsPage.featureVisibilityNoLevel: No level assessed yet — showing the Level 0-2 default preset.

sentryExample.connectivity: It looks like network requests to Sentry are being blocked, which will prevent errors from being captured. Try disabling your ad-blocker to complete the test.

commonsLicenseNotice.title: Commons license visible before publishing
commonsLicenseNotice.body: Knowledge you publish here defaults to CC BY-SA 4.0 unless the form offers a stricter choice. Share only material you have the right to contribute, and keep traditional or sensitive knowledge out unless its stewards have agreed.

governance.title: How Changemappers Can Be Challenged
governance.description: This page names the refusal, challenge, and accountability paths that hold the platform to its charter while fuller community governance is still being built.
governance.charterBody: Product decisions must respect dignity, consent, reciprocity, commons stewardship, plural knowledge, and honest incompleteness. Admin tools and recommendation systems are implementation details, not exceptions to that floor.

refusal.title: Refusal without penalty
refusal.body: You can restrict processing, opt out of algorithmic matching, withdraw sensitive matching consent, decline contact, or delete your account without losing basic access to public knowledge.

challenge.title: Challenge path
challenge.body: If a recommendation, moderation action, label, or governance decision feels wrong, use feedback or contact support and describe the affected page, decision, and harm. The team must name the reason and review path rather than silently close the issue.

power.title: Visible power
power.body: Admin-only controls are for safety, legal, and operational continuity. Route disabling, moderation, and data access should be documented and contestable when they affect a user or community.

limits.title: Honest limits
limits.body: Current V1 controls are broad. Some fine-grained label, temporal, lineage, and more-than-human controls remain planned work and must be described as such until they are shipped.

governance.openWorkBody: The permanent charter-holding body, amendment thresholds, and public refusal register still need community design. Until then, this surface is the minimum user-facing accountability layer.

charter.title: Universal Charter
charter.summary: The short governed commitments that anchor participation, consent, refusal, amendment, and stewardship.

sections.heading: Ten commitments
body: We protect refusal, informed consent, practical contribution, low-risk first exchange, commons stewardship, privacy, transparent limits, repair, plural knowledge contexts, and the right to leave.
The Charter cannot be amended casually. Amendments require public notice, steward quorum, and a dated version update.

sections.heading: Five-Year Governance and Power Review
body: Within five years of public launch, Changemappers must run a major public review of governance, founder advisory power, ownership, stewardship, and transition options.
This is a power review, not a disappearance promise: the outcome may be transition, sunset, stewardship restructuring, or a renewed mandate with reasons.

refusal-register.title: Refusal Register
refusal-register.summary: What Changemappers will not trade away under funder, partner, acquisition, or use-case pressure.

sections.heading: Current refusals
body: No sale of private matching data.
No public contribution scores or reciprocity leaderboards.
No funder control over match ranking.
No partnership that requires weakening refusal, block, or privacy controls.
No acquisition condition that removes the Charter amendment process.

challenge-path.title: Newcomer Challenge Path
challenge-path.summary: A plain route for correcting harm, incompleteness, or governance drift without knowing internal process language.

sections.heading: How to challenge
body: Name what feels wrong or missing.
Say who may be affected.
Suggest a safer wording, process, or limit if you have one.
Ask for a response from Charter stewards when the issue changes governance commitments.

what-we-cannot-yet-hold.title: What We Cannot Yet Hold
what-we-cannot-yet-hold.summary: A visible account of current platform limits across traditions, languages, work contexts, users, and product gaps.

sections.heading: Known limits
body: Many knowledge traditions are not represented by current metadata.
The interface is strongest in English and Hungarian and weaker for other languages.
Offline mutual-aid, trauma-informed, and high-risk organizing contexts need more safeguards than V1 offers.
Non-human stakeholder modeling is not implemented in V1.
Cases-with-Provenance has no verification workflow in V1.

defederation-protocol.title: Defederation Protocol
defederation-protocol.summary: The safety and exit process required before federation or remote-instance features are enabled.

sections.heading: Protocol
body: Triggers include repeated abuse, privacy violations, governance capture, or refusal-register breach by a remote instance.
Process requires evidence capture, notice where safe, user export/protection steps, reversible blocks when possible, and a public log.
A drill is mandatory before federation or remote-instance features can be enabled.

governance-roster.title: Governance Roster
governance-roster.summary: The initial public roster for role transparency until a formal governance-role data model exists.

sections.heading: Initial roster
body: Charter Steward body: three named founder/steward seats to be filled before public V1 launch.
Term ends are published here first; profile-level governance-role rendering is deferred until the data model exists.

tk-bc-labels.title: TK/BC Protocol Labels
tk-bc-labels.summary: Traditional Knowledge and Biocultural labels are protocol/context labels beside legal licensing.

sections.heading: Legal clarity
body: CC-BY-SA-4.0 remains the default commons license for public contributions.
TK and BC labels do not replace copyright licenses. They tell readers that origin, stewardship, consent, attribution, or place-based protocol matters.

term-limits-successors.title: Term Limits and Successors
term-limits-successors.summary: Default turnover and successor-mentoring expectations for governance roles.

sections.heading: Rules
body: Default steward terms are two years unless the roster states a shorter launch term.
Second-term role holders must name and actively mentor a successor or explain publicly why that is not yet possible.
Term exceptions require Charter steward review and dated publication.

peerSupport.title: Peer Support
peerSupport.metaDescription: Connect with people who have navigated similar situations, not therapy and not advice.

emailHelpPage.contactSuffix: from the address you are trying to use.

casesPage.empty: No cases have been published yet.

rootMetadata.description: A connection platform for people and communities seeking ecological, regenerative ways of living.

helpPage.title: Make informed choices on Changemappers
helpPage.intro: Search the core concepts behind registration, matching, profile visibility, and privacy controls before deciding what to share.
helpPage.noResultsBody: Try a broader word such as privacy, matching, availability, profile, consent, or map.

sections.title: A different kind of platform
sections.label: Philosophy
sections.summary: Changemappers is built on a different premise: useful relationships matter more than visible ones. There are no follower counts, no viral rankings, no attention economy.
items: There is no ranking by likes, star ratings, or reach metrics. Recommendations are based on fit and consent, not performance.
The platform is built around contribution, reciprocity, and reflection — not broadcasting. Popularity is not a signal the system optimizes for.
Every recommendation should be explainable: the system shows you people, causes, and communities because of shared context, not because someone paid for placement.

sections.title: What Changemappers is for
sections.label: Philosophy
sections.summary: Changemappers helps changemakers find the right people, communities, and opportunities for regenerative collaboration — locally and across networks.
items: The platform values useful, trustworthy relationships and shared growth over popularity or attention metrics.
Reflect, Discover, Connect, and Act are the four core movements: knowing where you stand, finding relevant others, building relationships, and doing meaningful work together.
The goal is to reduce wasted connections by asking for enough context — role, offers, needs, cause focus — to make recommendations meaningful rather than random.

sections.title: How to get started
sections.label: Getting Started
sections.summary: Start with account verification, identity setup, matching setup, and visibility choices. You do not need to fill everything in at once.
items: Create an account, verify your email, and complete the onboarding steps. Each step unlocks more of the platform.

The matching setup step collects the minimum context for useful recommendations: role labels, offers, needs, cause or RDG focus, contribution seed, and availability. Without it, you can browse but will not receive meaningful match suggestions.
Set profile visibility for location, avatar, profile fields, map presence, and what others can see — you can change these any time from profile settings.

sections.title: Finding your way around
sections.label: Getting Started
sections.summary: The app reveals more tools as they become relevant so the first session stays navigable — not because some features are more important than others.
items: New users start with essential discovery, reflection, profile, and connection tools visible in the menu.
More advanced coordination, systems, and governance tools appear as your use deepens, or can be enabled from settings.
This sequencing is guidance, not a value judgment. The interface is trying to reduce overload, not rank your development.

sections.title: Core actions in the app
sections.label: Getting Started
sections.summary: Most app activity fits into Reflect, Discover, Connect, and Act — four movements that build on each other.
items: Reflect: check in on your availability, focus, values, and energy before diving into external activity. This is the compass that keeps your participation intentional.
Discover: find people, communities, events, causes, stories, and map-based proximity that match your context and needs.
Connect: send requests, start messages, accept conversations, and build relationships that can carry real collaboration.
Act: offer or request training, mentoring, coaching, peer support, contributions, community work, and initiative support — routed to people who fit.

sections.title: How matching works
sections.label: Matching
sections.summary: The matching setup step collects the minimum signals needed for local recommendations: role, offers, needs, cause or RDG focus, contribution context, availability, and explicit consent.
items: Role labels (e.g. practitioner, researcher, organiser, funder) tell the algorithm what kind of collaboration you are likely to offer or need. Offers and needs are weighted highest in matching — they define the exchange surface.
Cause and RDG focus narrow geographic and thematic routing: the system tries to suggest people active in the same context, not just anyone with similar interests globally.

These are contextual routing signals, not permanent identity labels or public scores. Incomplete matching setup means you can still browse allowed surfaces, but you will not receive meaningful recommendations until it is complete.

sections.title: How availability shapes what you receive
sections.label: Matching
sections.summary: Availability tells the system whether to route new connection attention toward you right now. It is a protection mechanism, not a performance signal.
items: Between projects and Building are open states. The algorithm routes new recommendations and requests to you normally — you appear in match results for others.
Delivering reduces your recommendation weight. The system can still surface you, but with lower priority, because you are in active delivery and likely less available for new asks.

Reflecting and Resting are protection states. You may browse freely, but you will not be surfaced as a new recommendation to others. Incoming connection requests are also deprioritised. These are valid, intentional states — not failures or gaps.

sections.title: Safety, trust, and reporting
sections.label: Safety
sections.summary: Safety controls include reporting, role-gated admin areas, privacy controls, and availability protection. You are in control of your exposure.
items: Suspicious or harmful behavior can be reported from any profile or content card. Reports go to role-gated admin areas — not other users.
Sensitive fields (location precision, cause details, personal context) can stay private by design. Visibility controls independently shape profile, search, map, and card exposure.
Availability is part of safety: Resting and Reflecting prevent the system from routing new asks to you. No one can see your current availability state — only its effect on routing.

sections.title: Privacy controls and consent
sections.label: Privacy
sections.summary: Profile visibility and matching consent are related but separate controls. You can change either independently at any time.
items: Profile, search, map, and card visibility settings control what other people can see when they browse or search. Each surface has its own toggle.

Algorithmic matching consent controls whether your offers, needs, and role signals may be used to compute match scores. Sensitive matching consent is a separate, explicit opt-in for processing personal context fields.

From account privacy settings you can fine-tune visibility, restrict processing, export your data, request deletion, or withdraw sensitive matching consent. Withdrawal takes effect within the next matching cycle.

sections.title: Feed and signals
sections.label: Community
sections.summary: The feed shows what is happening across your network and relevant causes — ordered by time, not engagement score.
items: Feed order is chronological. There is no algorithm boosting posts by likes or reach — newer content appears first regardless of who posted it.
Reactions are typed and specific: inspired, learned, can help, can connect. These are contribution signals, not vanity metrics. They help the system understand what kind of value a post has.
RDG tags and curated cause annotations add geographic and thematic context to posts without turning the feed into open hashtag clutter. You can filter the feed by cause or community.

sections.title: Updating your context over time
sections.label: Matching
sections.summary: Your matching answers are adjustable as your situation, focus, and energy change. Low-resolution answers are fine at the start.
items: You can modify your role labels, offers, needs, cause focus, contribution context, and availability from your profile at any time. Changes are reflected in the next matching cycle.
Changing availability pauses or resumes how much new connection attention reaches you — useful when moving between projects, recovering, or shifting focus.
Deliberately vague answers are acceptable at registration. Refine them when you know more precisely what kind of help, collaboration, or contribution you are ready for.

sections.title: Learning and peer support
sections.label: Learning
sections.summary: Training, mentoring, coaching, peer support, and contribution are intentionally different kinds of help — each with a different relationship dynamic.
items: Training transfers a skill or method from someone with direct knowledge to someone who needs it. It is asymmetric and structured: there is a syllabus or defined outcome.
Mentoring offers judgment from lived experience. Coaching helps you find your own answer through questions rather than advice. Peer support is time-bounded mutual accompaniment between equals.
Completion is documented through reflection and learning notes, not numeric ratings or popularity scores. The focus is on what changed, not on performance.

sections.title: Choosing what to share
sections.label: Privacy
sections.summary: Share enough to make useful connections, but do not disclose more than you are ready to have used in recommendations or profile context.
items: Choose availability honestly. Resting and Reflecting are complete, valid states — not gaps or failures. Choosing them signals self-awareness, not disengagement.

Use needs and offers specific enough to guide matching without revealing private details you do not want processed. You can be specific about the type of collaboration without naming personal circumstances.
If a visibility or consent setting feels too broad for where you are right now, choose the narrower option and expand it later. Settings are reversible; disclosures are harder to undo.
